messages.content: Bob Dylan - Mr. Tambourine Man helped create the genre known as
folk rock.
who made you really got me know first van halen or the kinks
The Kinks originally released the song "You Really Got Me" in 1964, and it became a hit single. Van Halen covered the song in 1978, but the original version was released by The Kinks.
respect find out what it means to me what on what label

"Respect" is a song originally written and recorded by Otis Redding in 1965, but the better-known rendition was performed by Aretha Franklin, and it was released on the Atlantic Records label in 1967. Aretha Franklin's version of the song became a massive hit and is regarded as a seminal song of both the R&B and the feminist movements.
was mr. tambourine man written by bob dylan

Yes, "Mr. Tambourine Man" was written by Bob Dylan. It was originally released on his album "Bringing It All Back Home" in 1965. The song became a hit for the Byrds in the same year, reaching the top of the charts, and it has since become one of Dylan's most famous and iconic compositions.
what are the lyrics tomorrow never knows refer to eastern philosophy

Yes, the lyrics of "Tomorrow Never Knows" by The Beatles refer to elements of Eastern Philosophy. The song was written by John Lennon and it was heavily influenced by his interest in Indian mysticism and the teachings of the Maharishi Mahesh Yogi. The lyrics of the song contain references to various concepts from eastern philosophy, including the Tibetan Book of the Dead and the idea of the egoless state of mind found in Buddhist teachings.
is that the tibetan of the dead

Yes, "Tomorrow Never Knows" by The Beatles does make reference to the "Tibetan Book of the Dead", which is a Buddhist text that provides guidance on the spiritual journey that the soul undergoes after death. In the song, Lennon uses this reference to suggest the idea of letting go of the ego and the material world in order to achieve a higher state of consciousness. The lyrics of the song also contain references to other aspects of Eastern philosophy such as the Hindu concept of Maya and the opening lines reference the psychedelic experiences and otherworldly sensations of using LSD, which was a common theme in music of that era.

In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the British music industry was dominated by:
Group of answer choices

large companies and major labels

independent radio stations

independent record labels

weekly radio shows

In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the British music industry was dominated by large companies and major labels. Independent record labels also played a role in the industry, but they were not as dominant as the major labels. Independent radio stations and weekly radio shows also existed, but they did not have as much influence on the industry compared to the major record labels and companies. One of the major record labels in the UK during this time was EMI, which had significant influence over the industry and helped to establish many popular artists.
what were the original name for the beatles

The Beatles initially had several different names before settling on their famous moniker. In their earliest days, the band was known as The Quarrymen, named after the school attended by founding members John Lennon and Paul McCartney. Later, the band was briefly called Johnny and the Moondogs, as well as Long John and the Silver Beetles. They eventually became known simply as The Beatles, inspired in part by the name of Buddy Holly's band, The Crickets. The band's name is also a play on the word "beat," alluding to both the musical rhythm and their home city, Liverpool, which was known for its beat culture.

From the perspective of American youth, the British invasion was centered on:
Group of answer choices

a wide range of musical styles found in British bands

the combination of pop- and blues-oriented songwriting

nonmusical issues such as a band’s image and its members’ accents

American music being rewritten and repackaged for consumers

From the perspective of American youth, the British invasion was centered on a wide range of musical styles found in British bands. While some British bands drew heavily from the blues (such as The Rolling Stones), others were more pop-oriented (such as The Beatles). However, the overall focus of the British invasion was on the distinct sound and style that British bands brought to American audiences. Nonmusical issues such as a band's image and its members' accents certainly played a role in the popularity of some bands, but these factors were less central to the British invasion than the music itself. Similarly, while there was an element of American music being repackaged and rewritten by British bands, this was not the defining factor of the British invasion.

The period 1964–1967 at Motown was dominated by which writing and production team?
Group of answer choices

William “Mickey” Stevenson and William “Smokey” Robinson

Berry Gordy and Mary Wells

Valerie Ashford and Nick Simpson

Brian Holland, Lamont Dozier, and Eddie Holland

The period 1964-1967 at Motown was dominated by the writing and production team of Brian Holland, Lamont Dozier, and Eddie Holland. The team was known as Holland-Dozier-Holland and they were responsible for creating many of Motown's biggest hits during the mid-1960s. Some of their most successful songs included "Baby Love" and "Stop! In the Name of Love" by The Supremes, "Can't Help Myself (Sugar Pie Honey Bunch)" by The Four Tops and "Reach Out I'll Be There" by The Four Tops, among many others. Their distinctive sound and production techniques helped to define the Motown sound and made them one of the most successful songwriting teams in history.
